claims.evidence_text: On average, dryland spring wheat grown in Montana requires 3.3 lb available N/bu to reach 14 percent protein, where available N = soil N + fertilizer N
claims.claim_summary: Dryland spring wheat requires ~3.3 lb available N per bushel to reach 14% grain protein.

claims.evidence_text: dryland winter wheat requires an average of 2.3 lb N/bu to produce 40 bu/acre with 12.5 percent protein... when soil organic matter is 2 percent
claims.claim_summary: Dryland winter wheat requires ~2.3 lb N per bushel to produce 40 bu/acre at 12.5% protein (2% SOM).

claims.evidence_text: Grain protein is likely to increase with late-season N if the flag-leaf N concentration is less than 4.2 percent
claims.claim_summary: Flag-leaf N concentration below ~4.2% indicates grain protein will likely respond to late-season N.

claims.evidence_text: Most of the N used by wheat for grain protein is taken up before heading or flowering... Late-season N can be applied specifically to boost protein... applied anytime between boot and early-milk stages.
claims.claim_summary: N taken up before heading drives yield; late-season N applied between boot and early-milk stages boosts grain protein.

claims.evidence_text: dryland winter wheat... 40 bu/acre with 12.5 percent protein, and spring wheat requires... 40 bu/acre with 14 percent protein
claims.claim_summary: Target grain protein ~12.5% for winter wheat and ~14% for spring wheat in Montana dryland systems.

claims.evidence_text: A combination of foliar N (27 lb N/acre) and S (10 lb S/acre) applied at flowering was necessary to obtain protein quantity and quality that was not achieved with N or S applied alone
claims.claim_summary: Sulfur aids N uptake; combined foliar N (27 lb/acre) + S (10 lb/acre) at flowering improves protein quantity and quality.

claims.evidence_text: An Idaho study found 75 lb/acre of late-season N on irrigated wheat increased lodging and reduced yield
claims.claim_summary: Excessive late-season N (75 lb/acre) on irrigated wheat increased lodging and reduced yield.

claims.evidence_text: dryland winter wheat consistently had higher protein when grown after winter and spring pea grown for grain than after fallow or barley... Growing legumes before wheat should boost protein
claims.claim_summary: Growing wheat after legumes (pea) in rotation raises grain protein versus fallow or barley.

claims.evidence_text: an additional 20 lb N/acre was required under no-till versus conventional till to produce acceptable grain protein in the first six years after conversion
claims.claim_summary: No-till on medium/fine soils requires ~20 lb N/acre more than conventional till to maintain grain protein in early years after conversion.
